data.title: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Faces New Allegations of Secret Payments to Nursing Homes Amidst Pending Securities Fraud Suit – Hagens Berman
data.description:
SAN FRANCISCO, June 04, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Shares of UnitedHealth Group (NYSE: UNH), the nation’s largest health insurer, fell sharply on May 21, after a British newspaper reported that the company had made undisclosed payments to nursing homes in an effort to reduce hospital transfers, intensifying the scrutiny facing the healthcare conglomerate.

The allegations add to UnitedHealth’s woes, as the company had to grapple with the filing of a securities fraud class action, the abrupt departure of its CEO, and reports of criminal and civil probes on company's practices.

Hagens Berman is investigating the securities fraud claims alleged in the pending suit and urges UnitedHealth investors who suffered substantial losses to submit your losses now.

Class Period: Dec. 3, 2024 – Apr. 16, 2025

Lead Plaintiff Deadline: July 7, 2025

Visit: www.hbsslaw.com/investor-fraud/unh

Contact the Firm Now: UNH@hbsslaw.com

                                        844-916-0895

Allegations of Secret Payments and Patient Harm

On May 21, The Guardian reported that UnitedHealth deployed medical teams to nursing facilities across the United States and offered bonuses to limit hospitalizations among Medicare Advantage enrollees, as part of a wider cost-cutting initiative. According to internal documents and whistleblower accounts cited by the newspaper, these financial incentives sometimes resulted in delayed or averted transfers for patients who might have benefited from immediate hospital care. In one case, a patient reportedly suffered permanent brain damage after a delayed transfer for a suspected stroke.

The Guardian’s reporting has added to a series of setbacks for the company, which is already contending with regulatory investigations and legal challenges.

Pending Securities Class Action

The company is the subject of a securities class action suit in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, brought on behalf of investors who purchased UnitedHealth securities. The complaint alleges that the company engaged in a strategy of improperly denying health coverage to boost profits, drawing regulatory scrutiny and public criticism. The suit, captioned Faller v. UnitedHealth Group Incorporated, asserts violations of federal securities laws.

The complaint alleges that the truth emerged on April 17, 2025, when UnitedHealth significantly reduced its full year 2025 financial guidance which it attributed, in part, to “[h]eightened care activity indications within UnitedHealthcare’s Medicare Advantage business, . . . far above the planned 2025 increase” that “was most notable within physician and outpatient services.” On this news, the price of UnitedHealth stock declined more than 22%, from $585.04 per share on April 16, 2025, to $454.11 per share on April 17, 2025.

CEO Departure and Regulatory Scrutiny

Since the filing of the suit, turmoil has only deepened. On May 13, UnitedHealth announced the abrupt departure of its chief executive, Andrew Witty, and suspended its 2025 fiscal guidance, sending shares down more than 10 percent that day.

The Wall Street Journal has also reported that federal authorities are investigating whether UnitedHealth pressured clinicians to record diagnoses that would make Medicare Advantage patients appear sicker than they were, a practice known as “upcoding,” which can result in higher federal payments.

Hagens Berman’s Investigation

Shareholder rights law firm Hagens Berman is actively investigating the securities fraud claims alleged in the pending suit against UnitedHealth. Reed Kathrein, the partner leading the investigation, commented on the mounting scrutiny:

“The Guardian’s reporting raises new and troubling questions about UnitedHealth’s commitment to patient care and transparency. Investors deserve clear answers about whether cost-cutting measures came at the expense of patient well-being.”

If you invested in UnitedHealth and have substantial losses, or have knowledge that may assist the firm’s investigation, submit your losses now »

Whistleblowers: Persons with non-public information regarding UnitedHealth should consider their options to help in the investigation or take advantage of the SEC Whistleblower program. Under the new program, whistleblowers who provide original information may receive rewards totaling up to 30 percent of any successful recovery made by the SEC. For more information, call Reed Kathrein at 844-916-0895 or email UNH@hbsslaw.com.

About Hagens Berman

Hagens Berman is a global plaintiffs’ rights complex litigation firm focusing on corporate accountability. The firm is home to a robust practice and represents investors as well as whistleblowers, workers, consumers and others in cases achieving real results for those harmed by corporate negligence and other wrongdoings. Hagens Berman’s team has secured more than $2.9 billion in this area of law. More about the firm and its successes can be found at hbsslaw.com. Follow the firm for updates and news at @ClassActionLaw. 

Contact:

Reed Kathrein, 844-916-0895

data.title: Greif's Fiscal Q2 Non-GAAP Net Income, Sales Rise
data.description:
Greif reported late Wednesday fiscal Q2 non-GAAP net income of $1.19 per diluted share, up from $0.83 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $1.13.

Net sales in the three months ended April 30 rose to $1.39 billion from $1.37 billion a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $1.42 billion.

Greif shares rose 2.1% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Insmed Insider Sold Shares Worth $451,535, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
David W J McGirr, Director, on June 03, 2025, sold 6,250 shares in Insmed for $451,535. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, McGirr has control over a total of 97,723 common shares of the company, with 97,723 shares held directly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1104506/000162828025029370/xslF345X05/wk-form4_1749069680.xml

data.title: Canadian bill seeks to deny hearings to some asylum-seekers
data.description:
A Canadian border-security bill introduced by the Liberal government earlier this week may deny some asylum-seekers a refugee hearing and make it easier for the government to revoke migrants' status.

The bill comes as the government seeks to address U.S. concerns about its border security and reduce the number of migrants in the country. In addition to denying some refugee hearings and allowing the suspension, cancellation or variance of immigration documents, the bill facilitates sharing people's information and makes it easier to read people's mail, among other measures.

President Donald Trump has said Canada had failed to do enough to stem the flow of illicit fentanyl into the U.S., using that as justification for some of his tariffs. This week Trump doubled the tariffs in place on steel and aluminum, prompting calls for Canada to boost retaliatory measures of its own. Late last year Canada pledged C$1.3 billion to beef up its border.

As Canada reduces the number of new permanent and temporary residents, its refugee system faces a historic backlog of more than 280,000 cases.

This week's bill follows through on some of those border promises as well as on suggestions from some top ministers that Canada would fast-track refusals for some refugee claims.

If the bill passes, asylum-seekers who have been in Canada more than one year would not be eligible for refugee hearings.

Instead, they would have access to a pre-removal risk assessment, meant to determine whether they would be in danger in their country of origin. According to data published by Canada's Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Department, 30% of pre-removal risk assessments in 2019 for people deemed ineligible for refugee hearings were approved; by contrast, according to Immigration and Refugee Board data, that year 60% of finalized refugee hearings were approved.

Asylum-seekers who wait two weeks to file claims after crossing from the U.S. to avoid being turned back under a bilateral agreement would also not get hearings.

The bill, which needs to go through multiple readings before the House of Commons votes on it and sends it to the Senate, would also allow the government to "cancel, suspend or vary" immigration documents if deemed in the public interest.

Migrant and refugee advocates worry the changes could leave vulnerable people deported to dangerous situations in their home countries without adequate due process.

A spokesperson for Canada's Immigration Minister Lena Metlege Diab said on Wednesday that the government recognizes the conditions in people's home countries may change, but the pre-removal risk assessment will prevent them from being returned to persecution or torture.

"The asylum ineligibilities introduced yesterday seek to maintain protection for those fleeing danger while discouraging misuse that bypasses the asylum system's function – which is to protect the vulnerable," the spokesperson wrote in an email.

"Canada is reneging on its basic human rights obligations to do individual arbitration," said Migrant Rights Network spokesperson Syed Hussan.

"This is teeing up a deportation machine."

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1035 ET - EU merger enforcers should let European telecoms companies consolidate to enable them to compete globally, Justin Hotard, president and CEO of Finnish company Nokia, says at a conference in Brussels. "If we just simply allow European telecom operators to consolidate--in many countries, from four to three--like you've seen in the U.S., like you've seen in India. If we allow that to happen, what we'll see is greater scale," he says. "We'll unlock true investment in innovation and 5G and 6G," he says. Consolidation could also lead to faster investments in broadband and mobile, he says. "You have to allow a regulatory environment that permits scale." (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1035 ET - CrowdStrike's valuation leaves only limited upside from its current level, BofA Securities analysts say in a research note. The cybersecurity company swung to a loss in 1Q, and its revenue guidance for the 2Q came in below the Street's expectations. The analysts are positive on the healthy underlying trends in 1Q, with growth in annual recurring revenue and products outside the core offering improving. But its 2Q guidance is a negative, particularly as the analysts see growth decelerating on an annual basis looking out to 2027. The analysts downgrade CrowdStrike to neutral from buy but raise their price objective to $470 from $420. Shares decline 4.3% to $467.50. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0955 ET - J.P. Morgan analysts say in a research note that Hewlett Packard Enterprise delivered a positive surprise in the recent quarter, posting solid results and guidance. The server and cloud-software company highlighted better-than-expected artificial-intelligence server revenue in its F2Q. The company remains on track for a strong current quarter thanks to revenue realization from a large cluster deployment, as well as its selective deals strategy, the analysts say. At the same time, there is little evidence of slowdowns in HPE's non-AI segments. The recent report allows HPE sidestep "the high-competitive intensity around AI servers, as well as broader macro concerns that had been building up into the print," the analysts write.(connor.hart@wsj.com)

0945 ET - Raymond James says, "Hewlett Packard Enterprise has prioritized profits, particularly in the artificial-intelligence market," and this contrasts with its competitors. "We consider this a smart strategy that can play into its strength with enterprises and high-performance computing," the analysts write. The company continues to work through challenges in the business that hurt earnings in the recent quarter, the analysts note. Still, the company appears to be on the right track, and the challenges didn't hold the firm back from posting a beat-and-raise quarter. Hewlett Packard Enterprise rises 3% in early trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0603 ET - Germany's DAX rises to a new high in morning trade after the German cabinet approved Chancellor Friedrich Merz's tax plan to stimulate Europe's biggest economy. The German blue-chip index is up 1% at 24337.20 after rising as high as 24346.15, a new record. The German cabinet approved Wednesday a proposal to introduce corporate tax breaks and support the country's economy. Infineon Technologies, Airbus and DHL owner Deutsche Post top the DAX's gainers. The DAX last closed at a record level on May 27, when it ended the session at 24226.49. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0455 ET - S4 Capital's disclosure of T-Mobile as the name behind a previously announced technology client win is the most significant news in an update ahead of its annual general meeting, Panmure Liberum's Johnathan Barrett and Sean Kealy say. The U.K. digital advertising firm cut its full-year revenue expectations to a low-single-percentage-digit decline. However, Panmure Liberum had already forecast a 2% fall out of caution about the economic environment. "The only real new news is the clarification of T-Mobile as being the mystery client behind the significant technology win previously announced," the analysts say in a note. Shares rise 5.6% to 27.20 pence. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0141 ET - Prosus shares are likely to fall on Chief Investment Officer Ervin Tu's departure given his track record and strong understanding of capital markets, Citi writes. However, the bank says Tu's departure shouldn't be a surprise given the company's focus on its e-commerce portfolio, from a holding company. Prosus said Tu was stepping down from his role as CIO and group president but will remain an advisor to the group. Shares closed at 44.885 euros and are up 17% over the year to date. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0033 ET - JOYY's near-term outlook may be supported by the advertising business and strong shareholder returns, says Deutsche Bank Research analyst Leo Chiang in a research note. Non-live-streaming revenue growth is likely to accelerate in 2Q, particularly in terms of ads, driven by favorable seasonality and the company's increasing emphasis on this business, Chiang says. Still, the analyst lowers the FY 2025 revenue forecast by 3% on a weaker live-streaming outlook, but keeps the adjusted net profit estimate largely unchanged to reflect a better margin outlook. The investment bank says the company's strong shareholder returns are attractive. It maintains a buy rating with a target price of $60.00. The ADRs last traded at $7.03. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

1836 ET - CrowdStrike has an opportunity to expand its market thanks to agentic artificial intelligence because AI creates a bigger risk of cybersecurity breaches, the company said during an analyst call Tuesday. AI Agents have access to multiple internal and external data stores, applications, and business processes. They increase the size and severity of and speed at which an attack can happen. "CrowdStrike is uniquely positioned to secure the identity, the workload, the infrastructure, the data and underlying AI models themselves," Chief Executive George Kurtz said. "CrowdStrike will be the protector of autonomous agents." (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Guidewire Software saw strong results in both deal counts and sizes in its latest quarter, CFO Jeff Cooper says during the company's earnings call. Insurers appear more willing to make large commitments now that the company has established its position in the cloud market, he says. Maintaining momentum with large, multi-year deals in the company's key 4Q will be key to determining the company's outlook, he says. The company hopes to notch a third year of high-teens fully-ramped ARR growth for a third year in a row, he says.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1818 ET - Guidewire Software saw growing demand across each tier of the property and casualty insurance industry in its latest quarter, with particular strength from the high-end of the market, CEO Mike Rosenbaum says during the company's earnings call. The company closed 17 cloud deals in the quarter, with seven core system deals with Tier 1 insurers and three with Tier 2 insurers, he says. Insurers are continuing to look to replace rigid systems with more agile platforms that allow them to deploy new products and lines of business faster, he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1748 ET - HealthEquity executives are praising the proposed expansion to health savings accounts that were included in the tax-and-spending bill passed by the House of Representatives last month. They were in Washington D.C. last month to advocate for just such expansions, CEO Scott Cutler says on a call with analysts. "It was serendipity that the House released their draft of the budget bill while we were there," he says. The provisions would give up to 20 million more American families access to the benefits of HSAs, founder Steve Neeleman says on the call. The provisions still have a ways to go before being implemented, including approval from the Senate. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1046 ET - Natural disasters and migration patterns loom large over state budgets in the U.S. "We are seeing more disasters and we are seeing more expensive disasters," Conning's Karel Citroen says. "In part, that is because people have moved to areas that are more more exposed" such as Florida and Texas, he says. As a result, insurance costs have skyrocketed in these locations, increasing living costs and pushing people to move out, eroding the local tax base. In a recent study, Conning found that international immigration boosted population growth in Florida, Texas and Nevada, while Utah and South Carolina remained popular destinations for retirees and people fleeing higher costs elsewhere. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1005 ET - The future of European banking hinges on frictionless digital experiences and a shift towards an investment-savvy culture, ING Group's CEO says at the Money20/20 conference in Amsterdam. Steven van Rijswijk notes that there is strong mortgage demand in Europe fueled by economic growth and low unemployment. He also highlights the need for banks to empower everyday individuals to invest safely, even in small amounts. "It if we want to stimulate the economy, we also need to make sure that people invest, invest in companies, invest in SMEs," he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0951 ET - UniCredit's exit from its Russian subsidiary would represent a capital hit of around 24 basis points, Jefferies says in a note after the Italian press reported terms for a potential sale of the unit to UAE-based funds at a $1.2 billion valuation. The estimate calculated by analysts compares with a cost of an "extreme loss" scenario in Russia of around 80 basis points, as per disclosure by the Italian bank. "Despite the low asset valuation, we believe that an exit would remove an overhang on the stock and de-risk the bank's excess capital, which benefited from the Ruble revaluation in the last quarter," analysts write. UniCredit's recently increased stake in Greek lender Alpha Bank could fill the income gap left by a potential Russia exit, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0937 ET - CaixaBank's CEO views new fintechs and digital banks as an incentive for traditional banks to innovate, rather than a direct threat to their position. Competition from these agile players has prompted incumbents to develop efficient systems, Gonzalo Gortazar says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. He cites Spain's popular payments solution Bizum. "We have to be able to incorporate everything that the new players bring us, who are highly innovative, and at the same time combine it with our solidity and with our people," he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Large-scale cross-border retail banking mergers in Europe are generally unprofitable and complex, CaixaBank's CEO says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Despite having a central supervisory authority, varied tax, consumer protection, and commercial regulations create significant friction. This is compounded by differing insolvency regimes and structurally dissimilar national systems, Gonzalo Gortazar says. CaixaBank's own acquisition of BPI in Portugal generated value due to the regional convergence, he says. However, the broader scene for large deals across borders is quite blurry, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Swedbank's investor day didn't contain any big surprises and the overall message is reassuring, JPMorgan analysts write. The bank's targets are broadly in line with previous guidance and somewhat expected, the analysts add. Swedbank aims to maintain a return on equity of at least 15% by 2027 and a cost/income ratio of 40% or below. "We expect limited EPS changes with consensus already forecasting 39% cost/income." JPMorgan says focus remains on the usage of excess capital, with potential upside to consensus dividend expectations. However, ongoing probes into anti-money laundering shortcomings remain an overhang and more visibility could be required to discount more payout. Shares fall 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0802 ET - The decade-long inertia gripping Europe's banking and capital-markets union projects is detrimental to citizens and inhibits economic growth, CaixaBank's CEO says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. The stagnation of the deposit guarantee fund is due to outdated fears about southern European banks, despite the view among investors that these are highly attractive markets, Gonzalo Gortazar says. There is an enormous reluctance among regulators and governments to cede small parcels of sovereignty, which is preventing a true single market for capital and investment, he notes. This leads Europe to watch its savings flow abroad to more efficient markets like the U.S., undermining domestic investment opportunities and competitiveness. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0724 ET - Crypto markets have suffered headwinds from cautious market sentiment, says LMAX Group's Joel Kruger in a note. U.S. listed spot bitcoin ETFs record a third day of net outflows. Economic pressures, U.S.-China trade tensions and looming tariff hikes contribute to market uncertainty, removing some of the positives for crypto right now, he says. However, ETF outflows were somewhat offset by an accumulation of holdings by so-called bitcoin 'whales', which are individuals or organizations with large bitcoin holdings, Kruger says. Bitcoin and ethereum are therefore likely to remain range-bound. Investors await upcoming U.S. data for clues on future Federal Reserve policy and the impact of tariffs, he says. Bitcoin falls 0.2% to $105,628. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0719 ET - ING's operations in Spain benefit from being part of an international bank with a market cap nearing 60 billion euros while operating as a fintech in the country, the CEO for the Dutch bank's Spanish and Portuguese markets says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. ING entered Spain in 1999 as a telephone bank and is now almost fully digital, operating with only 29 branches nationwide. Alfonso Tolcheff explains that the group chose Madrid to establish a new technology hub and plans to create one thousand STEM jobs in the next four years, reinforcing its commitment to the region. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0631 ET - Unicaja's president says he found a strong impetus for change and moving forward after the bank's complex merger with Liberbank when he joined the company almost a year ago. The bank recently outlined a strategic plan for the three years to 2027, which aims to create a better entity that is more client-focused and has a simpler management structure. "We have many things to do and I always think that is positive, because if we didn't have anything to do...there wouldn't be much additional value contribution," Jose Sevilla says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Unicaja and Liberbank merged three years ago. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0618 ET - Being a part of the landscape and having deep local knowledge is key for smaller banks to be competitive, Ibercaja's president says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. This generates a web of connections, making the bank a determining actor when it comes to fostering economic, social and cultural development, Francisco Jose Serrano says. The bank has been around for a century and a half and has always aimed to place the client at the center of its operations, he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0609 ET - Internal simplification has been the cornerstone of Unicaja's strategy to foster client "closeness", the Spanish bank's president Jose Sevilla says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Proximity banking goes beyond physical presence to encompass digital accessibility and consumer choice, he says. Sevilla adds that the lender has focused on creating agility in processes and has streamlined its product catalog to 200 products from 2000 a year ago. This internal focus, which also extends to the simplification of the organizational structure, is a key comparative advantage against larger entities, he notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1506 ET - Oil futures give back some of this week's gains in a volatile session. The 4.3 million barrel drawdown in U.S. crude inventories was offset by bigger-than-expected increases in gasoline and diesel stocks. Aramco cut its official July selling price to Asia by 20 cents a barrel, but raised prices for Europe and the U.S., while a report that Saudi Arabia favors further large monthly OPEC+ output increases after July added downward pressure on oil. President Trump said he had a good conversation with Russia's Vladimir Putin, but not one that will lead to immediate peace in the Russia-Ukraine conflict. He also said the U.S. needs a definitive answer soon from Iran on a nuclear deal. WTI settles down 0.9% at $62.85 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.2% to $64.86 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1348 ET - Oil futures get little support from the bigger-than-expected 4.3 million barrel drawdown in U.S. crude oil stockpiles, which was accompanied by unexpectedly large increases in product stocks. "The big builds in gasoline and distillates were just head-scratchers, so much so that the market almost dismissed it out of hand," says Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group. A drop in exports could have contributed to the 5.2 million barrel build in gasoline stocks, but the 1.2 million barrels-a-day decline in gasoline demand is questionable and will likely be adjusted. "Demand doesn't swing like that in gasoline," Flynn says. WTI crude is off 1.2% at $62.62 a barrel. Nymex gasoline is down 2.4% at $2.0291 a gallon. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1152 ET - Brookfield Renewable Partners should still be able to generate good returns for investors despite losing support from the U.S. Raymond James' Frederic Bastien says, as drafted, Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act proposes to eliminate the clean electricity investment tax credits for facilities that don't begin construction within 60 days after the Bill's enactment, or that aren't operational by the end of 2028. But Bastien isn't worried for Brookfield. "This does not concern us one bit since as a large, technology agnostic, and globally diversified renewable operator and developer, BEP is not beholden to any one government subsidy," Bastien says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0910 ET - Oil futures slip into the red following two days of gains. Prospects of higher OPEC+ are offset in the near term by lower Canadian production due to wildfires and geopolitical tensions. "Our short-term view of WTI remains one in which trade is likely to confine further to about the $60 to $64 zone," Ritterbusch says in a note. A test of last month's futures high of $64.19 "cannot be ruled out if the dollar weakens further and the up-trend in the equities continues," the firm adds. WTI and Brent are down 0.6% at $63.04 and $65.26 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0458 ET - Repsol isn't in a hurry to list its upstream business, RBC Capital Markets analysts write in a note after speaking with the Spanish energy major's finance chief Antonio Lorenzo. Repsol is committed to preparing the business for an initial public offering in 2026 but any transaction will depend on market conditions, they write. Repsol appears to be considering all options. This includes merging with a public or private U.S. exploration and production company, they add. A key criteria for Repsol is maintaining control of the new entity and the internal focus is on governance structures within the division, they say. Shares trade up 0.4% at 12.20 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0219 ET - Seatrium appears to have secured a strong start to its financial year, UOB Kay Hian's Adrian Loh says in a research report. Its 1Q business update shows successful delivery of ExxonMobil's One Guyana floating production, storage, and offloading unit and completion of 45 repair and upgrade projects, the analyst notes. Also, the integrated shipyard recently won a floating storage regasification unit conversion contract from Norway's Höegh Evi, which will commence late 1H. These projects signal strong order intake and underpin Seatrium's multi-year revenue visibility up to 2031. The brokerage has a buy rating and target price of S$2.96 on the stock, which is up 1.00% at S$2.05. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1410 ET - TD Cowen says Uranium Energy Corp. continues to ramp-up its Christensen Ranch mine in Wyoming with new production areas coming online "that we expect will mark the transition to producer status in FQ4/25." Analyst Craig Hutchison says in a report that this coincides with the renewed focus on the US domestic nuclear supply chain including homegrown production. Hutchison adds that Uranium Energy is one of the "very few" pure-play uranium companies with near-term production in a politically stable jurisdiction with potential for additional US government support. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1025 ET - Canada's largest public sector labor union is calling on the federal government to counter President Trump's increased tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum and introduce investment protections. "These tariffs are killing investment in our steel, aluminum, and auto sectors, and we are already seeing the consequences in lost jobs and economic instability," Unifor National President Lana Payne says. The union wants retaliatory tariffs on U.S. steel and aluminum to match the 50% rate and new border measures to prevent foreign steel and aluminum from being dumped in Canada. It also wants a temporary halt to exports of strategic metals to the U.S. and the building of a stockpile reserve. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0858 ET - Canada arguably doesn't need to retaliate against the doubling of the U.S. tariff on imports of aluminum and steel to 50%, since America is doing enough damage to itself as it fights all its major trading partners, Scotiabank's Derek Holt suggests. The economist notes the average effective tariff rate on U.S. imports increases with this move to 12% on all goods and services, or 14.6% on just goods. "That's a severe terms of trade hit to the U.S. economy compared to around a 2% effective rate on all imports before all of this nonsense began." Prime Minister Mark Carney has indicated a preference for "intensive and live negotiations," though Holt says that hopefully doesn't include some "Golden Dome" fiasco at the expense of Canadian taxpayers. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0350 ET - Mining stocks lead the gains on the FTSE 100 index in early morning trade. The rise reverses Tuesday's fall when they were among the largest fallers after the OECD downgraded its forecast for global economic growth. U.K. investors look eager to shrug off U.S. trade drama, Hargreaves Lansdown's Matt Britzman writes. Meanwhile, gold and base metal prices ticked up in early trade, providing a lift to the stocks. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo traded up 2.6% while Chilean copper miner Antofagasta rose 2.5%. Anglo American and Glencore were both up around 1.9% while Endeavour Mining was up 1.5%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0250 ET - Aarti Industries' performance is likely to improve meaningfully, Axis Securities analysts say in a note. This performance is underpinned by the specialty chemicals manufacturer's strategic focus on operational efficiency and cost optimization, the analysts say. The Indian company is also intensifying its focus on developing new products, especially within the ethylation value chain, the analysts say. This is supported by Aarti Industries' recent production capacity expansions aimed at improving asset utilization and broadening its product portfolio. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of INR520.00 on the stock, which is up 0.7% at INR473.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0109 ET - There are several ways Glencore shareholders might benefit if the miner pursues a merger with Rio Tinto, say Citi analysts Ephrem Ravi and Paul McTaggart. Glencore would gain exposure to iron-ore earnings, and growth from copper and lithium, they say. That would give it a much bigger pool of marketing options. If Glencore carves off coal assets to marry its metals operations with Rio Tinto's, that metals business could enjoy a rerating, say the analysts. Shareholders would retain exposure separately to the lucrative energy assets. Australian investors could also get fully franked dividends. For Rio Tinto, the main benefit of a tie-up would be diversification away from iron ore, the analysts say. The steel ingredient today accounts for most of Rio Tinto's earnings. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2149 ET - Affin Hwang IB analysts see Malaysia's equities market remaining volatile in 3Q amid global uncertainties, including the end of the 90-day pause on U.S. reciprocal tariffs and ongoing geopolitical tensions. However, they remain positive on Malaysia's strong investment cycle, structural reforms, and data centre expansion. Affin Hwang keeps its overweight call on Malaysia's equities market and retains its end-2025 KLCI target at 1650. It replaces Maybank with CIMB Group as a top market buy on more attractive valuations, and adds Petronas Chemicals and Sime Darby to its sell list, citing their disappointing 1Q earnings. The benchmark KLCI is down 0.1% at 1502.36. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1658 ET - The Federal Reserve is holding down demand for steel used in construction and manufacturing by not cutting interest rates, Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves says. "The Fed is ridiculously late in acting," Goncalves says during remarks to reporters at a meeting of the American Iron and Steel Institute, a steel industry trade group. "For the steel industry, it's killing steel consumption." (robert.tita@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1215 ET - Rev Group heads for the exits for some of its product lines. The maker of specialty vehicles and related aftermarket parts says it will exit the non-motorized travel trailer and truck camper product categories through a sale of the Lance Camper business, and discussions with prospective buyers have begun. "As our only non-motorized business over time, it has become a less significant part of our overall strategic focus with scale that no longer aligns with the broader direction of our portfolio," CEO Mark Skonieczny says on an earnings call. He notes challenges from the geographic distance between this business located in Southern California and the core RV business in Indiana. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1208 ET - Rev Group saw minimal impact from tariffs in 2Q thanks to around three months of inventory as it entered the quarter. The maker of specialty vehicles and related aftermarket parts expects a $5 million impact within the RV segment in the second half of the year related to luxury van chassis imported from Europe. CEO Mark Skonieczny on an earnings call says that impact is related to purchases and commitments that were already in place, and the company has transitioned future purchases of the chassis to domestic plants. Rev's adjusted guidance includes a roughly $10 million second half impact of tariffs on its material spend largely within its specialty vehicles segment. Shares surge 15% to $42.78, on pace for a new all-time high. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0925 ET - Thor Industries expects a tough next two quarters. "We expect the fourth quarter of our fiscal 2025 and the first quarter of our fiscal 2026 to be challenging," says CEO Bob Martin. "The current economic uncertainty has led to downward pressure on consumer confidence and has negatively impacted retail pull-through." The maker of Airstream and Jayco RVs expects things to improve as uncertainty dissipates. The company is maintaining its outlook for the fiscal year, even as it recognizes uncertainty may grow depending on the macroeconomic environment. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0922 ET - Thor Industries is seeing stark differences in demand for RVs in North America and Europe. The maker of Airstream and Jayco RVs reports sales and shipments for both North American towable RVs and motorized RVs rising from a year earlier, partly due to an increase in price. Sales and shipments for Europeans RVs decline from the prior-year, however. Pressure on margins persists as it manages through softer retail and wholesale demand in the North American motorized and European segments, says COO Todd Woelfer. Thor rises 9% premarket on stronger F3Q results. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0900 ET - DHL's express unit is its most important division and Citi says the market underestimates the EBIT margin opportunity as volumes recover. The unit recorded an operating margin of 12.3% in 2024 and management has guided for a figure in the mid-teens by 2030. Citi suggests that as volumes recover, the unit can deliver a 13.4% EBIT margin in 2026 and 13.7% in 2027, versus company-compiled consensus of 12.6% and 13.7% respectively. "The express segment has shown resilience in the past few years of volume decline, and we are close to see a potential volume recovery." Citi upgrades the stock to buy from neutral and lifts its target price to 48 euros from 40 euros. Shares rise 1.4% to 40.15 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0603 ET - Germany's DAX rises to a new high in morning trade after the German cabinet approved Chancellor Friedrich Merz's tax plan to stimulate Europe's biggest economy. The German blue-chip index is up 1% at 24337.20 after rising as high as 24346.15, a new record. The German cabinet approved Wednesday a proposal to introduce corporate tax breaks and support the country's economy. Infineon Technologies, Airbus and DHL owner Deutsche Post top the DAX's gainers. The DAX last closed at a record level on May 27, when it ended the session at 24226.49. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0354 ET - Tata Motors' newly launched Harrier.ev electric vehicle is likely a competitive offering versus its peers', Nomura analysts say in a note. Overall positioning of the Harrier.ev is similar to Mahindra & Mahindra's attractive feature-loaded battery EVs with long ranges, the analysts note. Such products should help boost EV penetration in India this year. Sales of the Harrier's Internal Combustion Engine model has been around 1,500 units a month over the past few months, and Harrier sales should receive a boost with the EV launch. Nomura has a neutral rating and target price of INR799.00 on the stock, which is up 0.6% at INR707.95. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0246 ET - NIO may need to raise more funding for the rest of this year, Nomura analysts write in a note. The Chinese automaker keeps its plan to improve gross profit margin as its top priority rather than shipment volume. NIO faces challenges in its financials, Nomura says. The brokerage remains cautious on NIO's 2025 business plan to achieve strong sales growth with new model launches alongside cost-reduction efforts. If NIO can reach monthly sales of 25,000 units for each of its NIO and Onvo brands into 4Q, there could be re-rating opportunities ahead. Nomura maintains its neutral rating and target price at $5.00. Shares last closed at $3.53. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0037 ET - NIO might not break even before 2027, Bernstein analysts say in a note after the Chinese automaker delivered another quarter of weaker-than-expected results. The company's 1Q gross margin was 7.6%, which Bernstein called disappointing. Its vehicle margin deteriorated to 10.2% from 13.1% in the previous quarter due to operating deleveraging resulting from lower production volume, the analysts add. NIO's management still thinks it can break even in 4Q this year, but Bernstein holds a more conservative view given the intensifying competition in the SUV market. It cuts its ADR target price to $4.00 from $4.50. U.S.-listed NIO last closed at $3.53. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Costco Sales Rise 6.8% in May
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Costco recorded higher sales in May across all regions.

The warehouse retailer on Wednesday said sales increased 6.8%, to $20.97 billion, in the four weeks ended June 1.

Across all locations, same-store sales rose 4.3% last month. Comparable sales--which account for store openings and closings--increased 4.1% in the U.S. and 3.3% in Canada. Across all other international regions, they were up 6.6%.

E-commerce comparable sales jumped 11.6%.

Excluding changes in gasoline prices and foreign exchange, overall same-store sales rose 6%, with e-commerce up 12%.

The monthly sales come after Costco posted a higher profit and sales for its fiscal third quarter, though same-store sales missed Wall Street estimates.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: PVH CORP. /DE/ SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
PVH Corp., a global apparel company known for its iconic brands such as Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, reflecting both achievements and challenges faced during the period.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $1,983.6 million, reflecting a slight increase from the previous year's $1,951.9 million, driven by growth in the EMEA and Americas segments.
  • Gross Profit: $1,161.7 million, a decrease from $1,198.7 million in the prior year, impacted by increased cost of goods sold.
  • (Loss) Income Before Interest and Taxes: $(332.2) million, compared to $205.1 million in the previous year, primarily due to significant goodwill and intangible asset impairments.
  • Net (Loss) Income: $(44.8) million, a decline from $151.4 million in the prior year, largely due to noncash impairment charges.
  • Basic Net (Loss) Income Per Common Share: $(0.88), down from $2.63 in the previous year, reflecting the net loss incurred during the period.
  • Diluted Net (Loss) Income Per Common Share: $(0.88), compared to $2.59 in the prior year, consistent with the basic net loss per share.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company reported revenue from its key brands, Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, with Tommy Hilfiger generating $1,048.1 million and Calvin Klein $886.1 million during the reporting period. Heritage Brands contributed $49.4 million.
  • Geographical Performance: The EMEA region led with $927.7 million in revenue, followed by the Americas with $608.4 million, and APAC with $351.7 million. The Licensing segment contributed $95.8 million.
  • Sales Units: The company operates through various distribution channels, including wholesale, owned and operated retail stores, and digital commerce sites. Wholesale revenue was $1,071.3 million, while retail revenue, including stores and digital commerce, was $816.5 million.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates challenges due to macroeconomic pressures, including inflation and tariffs, which are expected to impact operations, particularly in the second half of 2025. The company is also focusing on its PVH+ Plan to drive efficiencies and invest in growth, aiming for annual cost savings of $200 million to $300 million by 2026.

SEC Filing: PVH CORP. /DE/ [ PVH ] - 10-Q - Jun. 04, 2025

data.title: Couchbase, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Couchbase, Inc., a leading provider of modern database solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of fiscal year 2025. The report highlights the company's financial and operational performance, showcasing growth in key areas despite ongoing investments and macroeconomic challenges.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $56.5 million, representing a 10% increase compared to the same period last year, driven primarily by growth in subscription revenue.

Gross Profit: $49.7 million, with a gross margin of 87.9%, slightly down from 88.9% in the prior year due to changes in the revenue mix and increased costs associated with the growth of the database-as-a-service offering.

Loss from Operations: $(18.8) million, an improvement from $(22.5) million in the prior year, reflecting ongoing investments in business growth.

Net Loss: $(17.7) million, compared to $(21.0) million in the prior year, indicating a reduction in losses as the company continues to invest in its growth strategy.

Net Loss Per Share: $(0.33), compared to $(0.42) in the prior year, reflecting improved financial performance despite ongoing investments.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: Subscription revenue, which includes license and support, accounted for 97% of total revenue, with a significant portion derived from the Enterprise Edition of Couchbase Server and Couchbase Mobile. The DBaaS offering, Couchbase Capella, is sold on a consumption basis and contributes to subscription revenue.

Geographical Performance: The United States remains the largest market, contributing $38.5 million to total revenue, while international markets contributed $18.0 million. No individual foreign country contributed 10% or more of total revenue.

Customer Growth: The company increased its customer base to 937 customers as of April 30, 2025, up from 807 customers as of April 30, 2024. This growth was driven by both new customer acquisitions and expansion within existing accounts.

Product and Service Offerings: Couchbase Capella, the company's DBaaS offering, is highlighted for its flexible pricing model and ease of operation, which is expected to drive future customer acquisition and revenue growth.

Operational Strategy: The company employs a land-and-expand model, focusing on expanding within existing customers by increasing the scale of deployments and addressing new use cases. This strategy is supported by a strong sales and customer success organization.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue investing in its offerings, personnel, and infrastructure to drive future growth. This includes expanding sales and marketing efforts and enhancing product features and functionalities.

Impact of Macroeconomic Conditions: Current macroeconomic conditions, such as inflation and foreign currency fluctuations, have led to longer deal cycles and increased budget scrutiny among customers. The company is monitoring these conditions closely as they may impact future business operations.

SEC Filing: Couchbase, Inc. [ BASE ] - 10-Q - Jun. 04, 2025

data.title: White House taps special ops vet for key Latin America post, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Michael Jensen will oversee Latin America policy at the National Security Council
  • White House has floated military action in Mexico to take out cartels

The White House has tapped a career special forces operative with experience in counterterrorism operations to oversee Latin America policy at the National Security Council, two U.S. officials said.

The appointment of retired Air Force commander Michael Jensen as senior director of Western Hemisphere affairs at the NSC follows President Donald Trump publicly floating the idea of sending troops into Mexico to battle drug cartels.

In February, the Trump administration designated six Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, a move that some Democrats and analysts believe could serve as a legal pretext for U.S. military action in Mexico or other foreign countries.

Jensen, who served in multiple special tactics groups over the course of more than two decades in the Air Force, had been nominated in February to serve as assistant secretary of defense for special operations and low-intensity conflict. The administration pulled that nomination in mid-May, according to a congressional notice that did not give a reason.

Jensen has no obvious Latin America policy experience, according to his LinkedIn profile and publicly available documents. A 2008 article written by an Air Force public affairs officer said Jensen helped manage a "high-value target hunt" in Afghanistan, during which he "guided 31 close air support and surveillance aircraft during a 5-1/2 hour mission, which disrupted al-Qaeda operations."

CBS reported in April that Jensen was a candidate for the post, though Jensen has only stepped into the position in recent days amid a broad shake-up at the NSC, said the U.S. officials, who requested anonymity because they were not authorized to speak to media.

The White House did not respond to a request for comment, nor did Jensen, who also serves as a special assistant to the president in his new role.

TROOPS TO FIGHT CARTELS

Trump said on the campaign trail that he might send troops to Mexico to battle drug cartels. During the final year of his 2017-2021 presidency, he privately floated the idea of launching missiles into Mexico, according to a memoir by his second defense secretary, Mark Esper.

In May, Trump said he had offered to send U.S. troops to Mexico to help Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum combat drug trafficking, an offer that she rebuffed.

Jensen's appointment comes at a fraught time for the NSC, which helps coordinate U.S. foreign policy across a large number of agencies.

In late May, dozens of staffers were cut in a purge, the latest and most dramatic in a series of cuts that started in March. Mike Waltz, the first national security adviser, was replaced by Secretary of State Marco Rubio in early May and Rubio now holds the top posts at both the NSC and the State Department.

In recent days, remaining NSC staffers have been told they will be more involved in implementing - rather than making - policy, according to several people briefed on conversations with top White House officials.

Several foreign missions, including those of major U.S. allies, have lost their counterparts at the NSC and are struggling to stay in close contact with the Trump administration, according to several foreign diplomats.

Still, the NSC has in recent days made some initial move to fill staffing gaps, according to three U.S. officials, with White House officials soliciting resumes from officials elsewhere in government.

Rubio has publicly urged the U.S. national security and diplomatic communities to renew their focus on Latin America, as the Trump administration attempts to crack down on narcotics trafficking and illegal immigration.

About the Funds

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund and Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II each have an investment objective to provide total return through a combination of capital appreciation and high current income. Virtus Investment Advisers, LLC, a registered investment adviser affiliated with Virtus Investment Partners, Inc., is the investment adviser to each fund and Voya Investment Management is the subadviser.

Fund Risks

An investment in a fund is subject to risk, including the risk of possible loss of principal. A fund's shares may be worth less upon their sale than what an investor paid for them. Shares of closed-end funds may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. For more information about each fund's investment objective and risks, please see the funds' annual report. A copy of the funds' most recent annual report may be obtained free of charge by contacting "Shareholder Services" as set forth at the end of this press release.

About Virtus Investment Partners, Inc.

Virtus Investment Partners is a distinctive partnership of boutique investment managers singularly committed to the long-term success of individual and institutional investors. We provide investment products and services from our investment managers, each with a distinct investment style and autonomous investment process, as well as select subadvisers. Investment solutions are available across multiple disciplines and product types to meet a wide array of investor needs. data.title: PG&E Files 8K - Other Events >PCG
data.description:
PG&E Corp. (PCG) filed a Form 8K - Other Events - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on June 04, 2025.

On June 2, 2025, Pacific Gas and Electric Company (the "Utility") entered into an Underwriting Agreement for the issue and sale of (i) $400,000,000 aggregate principal amount of 5.000% First Mortgage Bonds due 2028 (the "2028 Bonds") and (ii) $850,000,000 aggregate principal amount of 6.000% First Mortgage Bonds due 2035 (the "2035 Bonds", together with the 2028 Bonds, the "Mortgage Bonds"). On June 4, 2025, the Utility completed the sale of the Mortgage Bonds. For further information concerning the Mortgage Bonds, refer to the exhibits attached to this report.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1004980/000119312525134966/d847651d8k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1004980/000119312525134966/0001193125-25-134966-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Cargojet Declares Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
MISSISSAUGA, ON, June 4, 2025 /CNW/ - The Board of Directors of Cargojet Inc. ("Cargojet" or the "Corporation") has declared a cash dividend of $0.35 per common voting share and variable voting share for the period from April 1, 2025 to June 30, 2025. The record date for determining shareholders of the Corporation entitled to receive payment of the dividend of the Corporation shall be June 20, 2025 and the payment date for such dividend shall be on or before July 4, 2025. These dividends will be eligible dividends within the meaning of the Income Tax Act (Canada).

Cargojet is Canada's leading provider of time sensitive premium air cargo services to all major cities across North America, providing Dedicated, ACMI and International Charter services and carries over 25,000,000 pounds of cargo weekly. Cargojet operates its network with its own fleet of 41 aircraft.

Notice on Forward Looking Statements:

Certain statements contained herein constitute "forward-looking statements". Forward-looking statements look into the future and provide an opinion as to the effect of certain events and trends on the business. Forward-looking statements may include words such as "plans," "intends," "anticipates," "should," "estimates," "expects," "believes," "indicates," "targeting," "suggests" and similar expressions. These forward-looking statements are based on current expectations and entail various risks and uncertainties. Reference should be made to the issuer's most recent Annual Information Form filed with the Canadian securities regulators, and its most recent Annual Consolidated Financial Statements and Quarterly Financial Statements and Notes thereto and related Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A), for a summary of major risks. Actual results may materially differ from expectations, if known and unknown risks or uncertainties affect our business, or if our estimates or assumptions prove inaccurate. The issuer assumes no obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statement, whether as a result of new information, future events or any other reason, other than as required by applicable securities laws. In the event the issuer does update any forward-looking statement, no inference should be made that the issuer will make additional updates with respect to that statement, related matters, or any other forward-looking statement.

data.title: Willis Lease Finance Insider Sold Shares Worth $453,210, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Austin Chandler Willis, Director, Chief Executive Officer, on June 02, 2025, sold 3,450 shares in Willis Lease Finance for $453,210. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Willis has control over a total of 886,059 common shares of the company, with 199,744 shares held directly and 686,315 controlled indirectly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1018164/000090514825001993/xslF345X05/form4.xml

data.title: Performant Healthcare Insider Sold Shares Worth $587,818, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Lisa Im, Director, on June 03, 2025, sold 180,867 shares in Performant Healthcare for $587,818. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Im has control over a total of 1,229,413 common shares of the company, with 1,229,413 shares held directly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1550695/000155069525000040/xslF345X05/wk-form4_1749069219.xml
